name: Nancy Wilson
abstract: Nancy Sue Wilson (February 20, 1937 – December 13, 2018) was an American singer whose career spanned over five decades, from the mid-1950s until her retirement in the early 2010s. She was especially notable for her single "(You Don't Know) How Glad I Am" and her version of the standard "Guess Who I Saw Today".

quotes.text: But I loved George Shearing. He was precious. We did – when we worked at Carnegie Hall – I think it was – I don’t know who we were celebrating. Celebrating somebody’s birthday. I don’t think it was mine. Anyway, Shearing played the music that many years later exactly the way it was played on the record.
quotes.caption: Nancy Wilson recalls performing with George Shearing at his 80th birthday concert
quotes.doc_title: Nancy Wilson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

quotes.text: Oh it was the fact that Cannonball Adderley had kind of stepped out of the jazz thing, into the pop. Because this was a huge across the board album. It was not just a jazz album. “The Masquerade Is Over,” “Sleeping Bee,” these songs just popped out everywhere. And that was the good thing about radio in those days and music is that the focus wasn’t so narrow then. We were able to play concert venues, Carnegie Hall where we were also able to go into, the south side of Chicago and play The Sutherland. So you could do so many more things then than you can today. The labels kind of keep you out of places. Whereas before we tried to broaden the scope. We, I believe that Cannonball Adderley took jazz out of the sawdust and he was one of the more commercial jazz artists. And he made his audience understand what he was doing.
quotes.caption: Nancy Wilson recalls Cannonball Adderley's commercial success and broad appeal.
quotes.doc_title: Nancy Wilson interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, November 16, 1995

quotes.text: That’s John Levy. I love him so, I just love him. So that worked out very well. And I came back from Australia in December of ‘60 and was married in California. I came here and moved to California. I really prefer it. I’m from a rural atmosphere. I’m from basically outside of the city limits. I’m not an inner city person. I can’t deal — I came to New York and the first year it was oh my God these tall buildings and where is the sun, where is the grass, I want to see a tree that is not in Central Park. So I have, it was quite an adjustment for me from the midwest coming to the East Coast. I basically have loved California. And I love the south. I could live in Atlanta. I could live in North Carolina. I like that. But I think I’m forever in California. I’m a desert rat although I’m basically Pisces and I love the water, I do live in the desert. And it’s probably because it’s more economical. I lived at the beach for years, and then I moved my family to the desert. My husband and I used to have two places at the desert, but the girls and I are back at the beach. So we just have a, I’ll tell you I’ve really been blessed. I’m able to be ten minutes from The Strand, which is one of the few places that has a room that’s really amenable for work and good for working. I can take the trio in and we can have a good time in there. I still play Carnegie Hall. I’ll be back in New York at the Blue Note actually in the new year again here at the Blue Note, and stay for the week after and do six nights. But I do enjoy New York to play. There’s an excitement here you can’t find anyplace else. So I do love Carnegie Hall. I have a good time here. Don’t want to live here. I want to come here and party, sing, have a good time.
quotes.caption: General statement about enjoying performances at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Nancy Wilson interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, November 16, 1995

quotes.text: Yes, we sang Ferris Wheel at Carnegie Hall, and Carl and Nancy left the building. I’ve never been that involved in the music. We were not there. We were someplace . . .
quotes.caption: Wilson recalls attending a concert where Ferris Wheel was performed
quotes.doc_title: Nancy Wilson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Hubert Laws
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewer mentions 'Nancy Wilson' in a list of artists, but the interviewee only responds to the subsequent mention of 'Chick Corea'.
about_quotes.doc_title: Hubert Laws — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Dawoud Sabu Adeyola
about_quotes.evidence: my very first jazz concert ever that I went to was at Kleinhans Music Hall, which was the Cannonball Adderley Sextet with Nancy Wilson.

about_quotes.speaker: Sheryl Bailey
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee does not mention Nancy Wilson; the interviewer mentions her in the context of DownBeat magazine covers.
about_quotes.doc_title: Sheryl Bailey interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, July 24, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Roy McCurdy
about_quotes.evidence: I’m still working with Nancy Wilson. You know I’ve been doing her, working with Nancy for sixteen years.
about_quotes.doc_title: Roy McCurdy interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, November 16, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Mickey Roker
about_quotes.evidence: So then I got the gig with Nancy Wilson... Me and Buster Williams was the rhythm section
about_quotes.doc_title: Mickey Roker interviewed by Michael Woods, Majesty of the Seas, May 31, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: John Levy
about_quotes.evidence: I work with because he’s with Joe and anything I can do to work with him is fine... I have a contract, a Nancy Wilson contract that has 40 pages on it, for Columbia Records.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Levy interviewed by Monk Rowe, Majesty of the Seas, May 31, 1995
